segments.s564-g17: چو introduces a temporal clause (“when”). یک سال نزدیک is a duration phrase meaning “nearly/approximately one year” (numeral + noun + modifier). طایر is the subject and بماند (3rd singular past/epic imperfect of ماندن) means “remained/lingered”; the time phrase precedes the verb in typical Classical Persian order.
segments.s564-g18: ز here means “from/of/among” in a poetic register; اندیشگان is the group affected (“the thoughtful/the wise”). دل به خون در نشاند is a causative-style clause: دل (heart) + به (into/toward) + خون (blood) + در (in) + نشاند (archaic past form used causatively/put/placed) — literally “set the hearts into blood,” i.e. “made their hearts bleed.” Word order is typical: agent/marker → affected group → result phrase.
segments.s564-g19: ز طایر is a prepositional phrase “from the bird.” یکی دختش is “one maiden/daughter of it” (یکی + noun with the possessive clitic -ش). آمد is the past verb “came.” چو ماه is a simile placed after the verb: “like the moon.”
segments.s564-g20: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past “you said” (گفتی introduces reported speech). که is the complementizer “that.” The following predicate (نرسیست) functions as the reported content; با تاج و گاه is an instrumental/associative phrase “with crown and time/occasion,” coordinated by و. Note: گفتی here is the verb “you said” and that sense is not listed in the provided lemma senses; نرسیست is the reported predicate (the lexical senses given in the wordlist do not match the verbal/nominal use here).
segments.s564-g21: پدر is the subject “father.” مالکه is the name/title given (here used as a name/title). نام کردش is a verbal phrase: نام (name) + کردش (did/made + 3rd‑person object clitic) → “named her.” چو دید = “when he saw.” Word order: subject → object‑verb → temporal subordinate clause.
segments.s564-g22: که introduces the content of his seeing: دختش “his daughter” is the subject; همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here meaning “indeed/just”; مملکت را is the definite object “the realm” (را marks the definite object); سزید is the past of سزا/سزايدن meaning “to deserve / befit.” The clause reads “that his daughter indeed deserved the realm.”
segments.s564-g23: چو = “when.” شاپور را سال شد بیست و شش is a classical construction for age: X را سال شد N = “X became N years old.” را is the object/topic marker attached to the person whose age is stated; شد is the past auxiliary “became.”
segments.s564-g24: مهی‌وش کیی is a proper name + epithet (Kayi = Kayanian). گشت is the past of گشتن used here in the sense “became”/“turned into.” خورشیدفش is an epithet/name (predicate nominative). The whole segment states that Mahīwush Kayi became known/ascribed the name Khurshidfash.
segments.s564-g41: حصاری شدند is a past passive/experiential construction: شدند (3rd pl. past of شدن, “became/was made”) with حصاری (from حصار) meaning 'enclosed/under siege' — together 'were besieged' or 'became enclosed'. Word order is verb + subject/topic (شده اند then آن سپه), and در یمن is the locative phrase 'in Yemen'.
segments.s564-g42: خروش آمد is a simple past statement: آمد (3rd sg. past of آمدن, “came/rose”) with خروش 'a cry/uproar' as subject, i.e. 'a cry arose'. از + list (کودک و مرد و زن) marks the source/agents of the cry; the singular nouns stand collectively for 'children, men and women'.
segments.s564-g43: بیاورد is a past-tense verb ('he brought' / 'he caused to come') placed before the subject (typical poetic inversion). شاپور is the subject; چندان سپاه is the object phrase meaning 'so many troops' or 'so great a host' (چندان here = 'so much/so many').
segments.s564-g44: که introduces a result/consequence clause ('so that...'). بر مور و بر پشه is a hyperbolic genitive/locative phrase 'even on/for ants and gnats'; بربست is the past transitive 'he closed/blocked' (بستن with poetic prefix بَر-), and راه at the end is the object 'the road/way' — together: 'so that he closed the road even to ants and gnats.'
segments.s564-g45: ورا is a pronominal adverb 'upon/against it' (referring back to the place/object); با سپاهش = 'with his army' (سپاه + possessive suffix ش = 'his army'). به دژ در is a locative phrase 'at the fortress gate' (دژ 'fortress', در here 'gate/door'), and بیافت is an archaic/poetic past form 'fell upon/attacked' (from بیافتن/افتادن). Word order places the object/pronoun first for emphasis.
segments.s564-g46: در جنگ = 'in battle'; و راه گریزش links 'and his way of escape' (گریزش = 'flight/escape'); نیافت is the negative past 'he did not find' (نِیافت = did not find/was unable to find). Together: 'in battle he found no way of escape.'
segments.s564-g47: شب و روز is an adverbial pair 'night and day'. یک ماهشان literally 'one month‑their' uses the possessive suffix -شان to indicate duration belonging to them ('for a month'). جنگ بود is 'there was war' / 'fighting lasted' (بود here = past 'was'). The whole segment: 'Night and day their fighting lasted a month.'
segments.s564-g48: سپه را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker ('as for the army'). به دژ 'at the fortress' is locative. بر علف تنگ بود is an idiomatic phrase: تنگ 'scarce/narrow', بر علف 'with respect to grass/fodder', and بود 'was' — 'their grass was scant' = 'they had little fodder.' Word order places the topic up front and the comment after.
segments.s564-g25: به دشت آمد is a simple past clause: به + noun + آمد = 'came to/into the plain' (به here = 'towards/into'). The conjunction و then links the next clause; لشکرش را بدید places the definite object (لشکرش) before the past verb بدید with the object marker را, giving 'he saw his army.'
segments.s564-g26: ده و دو هزار is a numerical phrase meaning 'twelve thousand' and functions as the direct object. از یلان ('from/out of the heroes') specifies the source, and برگزید is the past tense 'he chose/selected.' The preposition از takes the subpartitive sense here.
segments.s564-g27: ابا is a proper name (not the lexical senses listed) so sense is marked -1. هر یکی = 'each one' (quantifier + pronoun). بادپایی modifies هیون (modifier before head) meaning 'a swift-footed beast/mount.' The whole phrase reads 'Aba, for each one, a swift-footed mount...' with the main verb of distribution implied or elliptical in the next clause.
segments.s564-g28: به پیش here is adverbial 'ahead' or 'in front,' and اندرون = 'the interior/inner area.' صد رهنمون = 'a hundred guides' (numeral + noun). مرد is 'men' (the people concerned). The clause is telegraphic/elliptical (no explicit verb); the sense is that Aba placed/sent a hundred guides ahead into the interior for the men.
segments.s564-g29: هیون برنشستند is a past plural verb phrase 'the beasts mounted / sat upon (their mounts).' The coordinating و links the next unit; اسپان به دست literally 'the horses in hand' (به دست = 'in hand/held by hand'), a phrase describing the horses being led or held.
segments.s564-g30: برفتند is past plural 'they went/advanced.' گردان خسروپرست is a noun phrase 'the Khosrow-devoted troop/division' that follows the verb (poetic word order); together the clause means that that royalist troop moved off or advanced.
segments.s564-g31: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (ازان = 'from that/thereupon', پس = 'after/then'). ابا is the proper name (sense -1). ویژگان is plural 'the distinguished/selected ones'; برنشست is past 'he sat (down)/took his seat.' The clause means 'After this, Aba sat among the distinguished.'
segments.s564-g32: میان = 'among/in the midst,' کیی refers to the Kayanian (the Kayan dynasty/forces). تاختن is the infinitive 'the charging/attack,' and را is the definite object marker. ببست is a past transitive 'he closed/shut off' (here idiomatically 'he stopped/checked' the charge). The clause therefore means 'he stopped the Kayanian charge.'
segments.s564-g33: برفت is a past tense verb meaning 'went off/left' (here the archaic verb form with the prefix بر-); it appears before its complement for poetic inversion. از پس is a compound prepositional phrase 'from/behind' (از + پس) and شاه غسانیان is a genitive phrase 'the king of the Ghassanids' serving as the locative source ('from behind the Ghassanid king').
segments.s564-g34: This is a noun phrase giving the subject/name: سرافراز is an epithet 'triumphant/lofty' modifying the compound name طایر هژبر ژیان. طایر is a poetic epithet 'bird' (metaphorical), هژبر is a proper-name element, and ژیان is the second name-element; together they form the hero's epithet/name.
segments.s564-g35: فراوان کس (literally 'many persons') is a quantificational noun phrase ('many men'). از لشکر او marks those persons as 'from his army' (از = from; لشکر او = his army). بکشت is an archaic past verb 'killed' (here with the subject understood as the previously named man).
segments.s564-g36: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. طایر is the subject 'the bird'. چنان دید combines demonstrative/adverb 'thus/that' + دید 'saw' (past). بنمود پشت is a verbal phrase 'he showed/turned (his) back' (بنمود is an older past form of 'show/turn', and پشت = 'back' meaning he retreated).
segments.s564-g37: برآمد + خروشیدن is a common poetic collocation: برآمد 'arose/issued forth' governs the verbal noun خروشیدن 'a cry/roaring', so the whole means 'a roar arose.' داروگیر stands as a noun placed after this clause (here likely a nominal mention—its exact role is contextual).
segments.s564-g38: ازیشان is the prepositional phrase 'from them' (از + ایشان). گرفتند is the past plural 'they seized/took'. چندی is an indefinite quantifier 'some/several' modifying اسیر 'prisoners/captives'—together: 'they took some prisoners from them.'
segments.s564-g39: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('whose/that'). اندازه آن = 'its measure/number/extent.' ندانست is the past negative of 'know' here ('did not know'), and کس (with the negative sense licensed by ندانست) means 'no one.' The clause therefore means 'whose extent/number no one knew.'
segments.s564-g40: برفتند is past plural 'they departed/went away.' آن ماندگان is the subject 'those who remained' (the survivors). زان سپس is an adverbial phrase 'thereupon/after that' placed at the end for emphasis; together: 'then the survivors departed.'
segments.s564-g9: به تاراج داد is a light-verb construction: به + noun (تاراج) + داد (gave) literally 'gave to pillage', i.e. 'laid to the sack/raped and pillaged.' آن همه بوم و بر is a demonstrative + quantifier + compound noun (بوم و بر = 'land and settlements'); the whole phrase is the object of داد.
segments.s564-g10: کرا is an archaic relative/indefinite ('whoever' / 'who'), بود is the past copula 'was', با او is a prepositional phrase 'with him', and the trio پی و پا و پر enumerates kinds of companions/modes (followers/on foot, feet/hooves, and wings) — placed after با او as an apposition to indicate who/what accompanied him.
segments.s564-g11: ز is the poetic preposition 'from', پیوند here means 'lineage/kinship' or 'bond', and نرسی most naturally reads as a proper name (Narsi) in genitive position modifying پیوند (i.e. 'the line of Narsi'); یکی یادگار = 'one memento/keepsake' (one thing that remained). The ezāfe linking is poetically compressed.
segments.s564-g12: کجا is an interrogative adverb 'where', نوشه is a small drink/draught, and بد نام is a compound adjective 'ill‑famed' (بد modifying نام 'reputation'), so نوشه بد نام آن نوبهار = 'the ill‑famed draught of that new‑spring' (a rhetorical/questioning line).
segments.s564-g13: بیامد is simple past 'came', followed by the prepositional phrase به ایوان 'to the porch/iwan'. آن ماه‌روی is the demonstrative + epithet 'that moon‑faced (one)' serving as the subject; placing the subject after the verb is a poetic/marked word order for emphasis.
segments.s564-g14: همه طیسفون is 'all Ctesiphon' (همه = everyone/all; طیسفون = the city). گشت is past of گشتن/گشت (here 'became'), and پر گفت‌وگوی means 'full of speech/conversation' (پر = 'full (of)', followed by the noun گفت‌وگوی as the complement).
segments.s564-g15: ز is the preposition 'from', ایوانش = 'his/its iwan' (possessive suffix), بردند is 'they took/carried away', و کردند links the two verbs 'and made', and اسیر is the object/predicate nominal 'captive' (they 'made [him] captive'). The sequence follows a typical Persian verb-final complex where verbs appear after their objects in poetry.
segments.s564-g16: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that', دانا نبودند is 'they were not wise' (negated past copula), و links the coordinate predicate دانش‌پذیر which here is understood under the same negation: 'nor teachable/receptive to learning.' This elliptical coordination (negation applying to both predicates) is common in Persian.
segments.s564-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. یک چند is a poetic expression meaning 'a short while' or 'a few days'. بگذشت is past tense (3rd sg.) meaning 'passed' (time passed); بر شاه روز literally 'upon the king day' — poetic word order where بر marks the person affected ('for the king') and روز names the time (i.e. 'days passed for the king').
segments.s564-g2: فروزنده شد is a two-part predicate: فروزنده (an adjective/participle 'illuminating, radiant') + شد (past of شدن 'became'). In poetry the predicate comes before the noun it qualifies. تاج گیتی فروز is the subject noun phrase; گیتی‌فروز is a compound epithet ('world‑illuminating') modifying تاج ('crown').
segments.s564-g3: ز is the poetic preposition 'from', marking origin. غسانیان names the Ghassanids (a people). طایر here is a figurative noun 'bird' used as a poetic epithet for a person (messenger/hero). شیردل follows as a compound epithet 'lion‑hearted' modifying طایر.
segments.s564-g4: که is the relative 'who/which'. دادی is past tense 'he gave' (3rd sg.). فلک را is the direct object with the object marker را ('the sky/heaven'). به شمشیر دل uses به instrumentally here ('by/with the sword of the heart') — literally 'gave the heavens to/into the (power of) the sword of his heart', i.e. made heaven submit to his heart's sword.
segments.s564-g5: سپاهی is the head noun 'an army/host'. The list uses two synonymous prepositions (ز and از) for poetic variety: ز رومی = 'from the Romans' and از قادسی = 'from the Qudsi' (قادسی here meaning 'of Jerusalem' / 'from Quds'). No finite verb appears in this nominal list—the verb is supplied by context.
segments.s564-g6: ز بحرین = 'from Bahrain'; و از کرد = 'and from the Kurds' (کرد here is an ethnonym, not the verbal lemma printed in the glosses); وز پارسی should be read as و + ز 'and from' + پارسی 'Persians' (the manuscript/print merges و and ز into وز). This segment continues the geographic/ethnic catalogue of troops.
segments.s564-g7: بیامد is the simple past 'came' (3rd sg.). به پیرامن طیسفون is a prepositional phrase 'to the environs/outskirts of Tisfon (Ctesiphon)', where پیرامن means 'around/environs' and طیسفون is the place-name.
segments.s564-g8: سپاهی begins a noun phrase resumed here: 'an army'. ز اندازه بیش is a compressed poetic expression meaning 'beyond measure' or 'greater than measure' (ز + اندازه + بیش = from/than measure more → 'exceeding measure'). اندرون 'inside/within' indicates these were interior troops or 'an inner force'. Word order is characteristic of older/poetic Persian where quantifying modifiers follow the noun.

sentences.s564-s9: When the bird remained nearby for nearly a year, it made the hearts of the thoughtful bleed.
sentences.s564-s10: From the bird came a maiden, like the moon; you said that … with crown and time.
sentences.s564-s11: Her father named her 'Malakeh' when he saw that his daughter was worthy of the realm.
sentences.s564-s12: When Shapur reached his twenty‑sixth year, Mahīwush the Kayanian became Khurshidfash.
sentences.s564-s21: That host was besieged in Yemen; a clamor rose from child and man and woman.
sentences.s564-s22: Shapur brought so large a host that he blocked the road even for ants and gnats.
sentences.s564-s23: He fell upon it with his army at the fortress gate; in battle he found no way of escape.
sentences.s564-s24: Night and day their fighting lasted a month; the army, at the fortress, had scant grass for fodder.
sentences.s564-s13: He came out onto the plain, saw his army, and selected twelve thousand of the heroes.
sentences.s564-s14: Aba gave each one a swift-footed mount and sent a hundred guides ahead into the interior for the men.
sentences.s564-s15: The beasts mounted, and the horses were held in hand; the Khosrow-devoted troop moved off.
sentences.s564-s16: After that Aba took his seat among the distinguished, and he checked (closed off) the Kayanian charge.
sentences.s564-s17: The triumphant bird Hējbar Zhiyān left from behind the Ghassanid king.
sentences.s564-s18: He killed many men of his army; when the bird saw this he showed his back.
sentences.s564-s19: A roar arose; they took some prisoners from them.
sentences.s564-s20: No one knew the exact number of them; then the survivors departed.
sentences.s564-s5: He laid all those towns and lands to the sack; whoever was with him—whether followers on foot, on hoof, or with wings.
sentences.s564-s6: From the lineage of Narsi one keepsake remained; where is that ill‑famed draught of the new spring?
sentences.s564-s7: That moon‑faced one came to the iwan; all of Ctesiphon became full of conversation.
sentences.s564-s8: They took him from his iwan and made him captive, for they were neither wise nor receptive to learning.
sentences.s564-s1: When a short while had passed for the king, the world‑illuminating crown became radiant.
sentences.s564-s2: From the Ghassanids came a lion‑hearted bird — who made heaven bow to his heart's sword.
sentences.s564-s3: A host from the Romans and from the Qudsi, from Bahrain and from the Kurds and Persians.
sentences.s564-s4: A host came to the outskirts of Ctesiphon; an inner force beyond measure.
